goal: Create an 8-week (≈2 months) manual driving lessons plan with 16 lessons (90 minutes each) plus 1–2 short private practice sessions per week, aiming to be test-ready in ~2 months.

Plan now includes measurable milestones and a two-mock-test readiness framework to determine when to book the actual driving exam; booking to occur after consistently meeting the milestones per the consistency rule.
Incorporate basic roadside breakdown skills (jump-starts and tyre checks) into the planned lessons with concise steps and practice scenarios.

Plan prioritizes slow-speed control as the foundation, with lane discipline introduced after establishing consistent slow-speed performance (Stage 1 slow-speed drills; Stage 2 lane discipline; Stage 3 traffic exposure).
constraints: Frequency: two weekday evenings per week
Milestones must be demonstrated consistently (2–3 sessions on different days) before booking
Starting practice in a quiet space before public roads (to build foundational skills)
Weekend slots are unpredictable; prefer to minimize weekend lessons and rely on weekday evenings.
Inclement weather: postpone lessons in snow/ice or heavy rain for safety; plan wet-weather drills in safe conditions.
Between-lesson practice on a familiar commute route to build route-specific familiarity and confidence.
Include basic roadside breakdown skills (jump-starts and tyre checks) as core practice within the plan.
decisions: Milestone-based assessment and mock-test framework integrated into plan
Incorporate quiet-space initial practice as recommended
Consider evaluating tech-assisted feedback tools and app integration into the plan
Adapt plan into a flexible, weekday-focused calendar to avoid weekend commitments
Add preference for calm, patient instructor and trial lesson to assess compatibility
Integrate breakdown skills training (jump-starts, tyre checks) into plan.
Adopt staged progression prioritizing slow-speed control before lane discipline (Stage 1 slow-speed drills; Stage 2 lane discipline; Stage 3 traffic exposure).
entities: 8-lesson plan (one-week spacing)
Worries: traffic, dusk/night driving, reversing/parking, independent driving
Printable one-page checklists for each lesson (optional deliverable)
Milestones (pass/fail gates) and mock-test framework
Two full mock tests (separate days) with predefined pass criteria
Quiet-space initial practice concept (empty car park)
Tech-assisted driving feedback app (records trips, analyses steering smoothness, etc.)
Flexible drills that can be integrated into errands
Flexible calendar approach (weekday-focused) to avoid weekend commitments
Habit awareness: wheel-tapping habit observed in father; potential distraction
Calm, patient instructional style; trial lesson to assess compatibility
Dual controls (instructor's car with extra pedals)
Logbook (training record)
Hazard perception
Turn-in-the-road / three-point turn
Emergency stop
Weather-related driving drills (wet roads, snow/ice) and safety protocols
Weather readiness checklists (tyres, wipers, screenwash, lights, visibility)
Familiar-route practice on commute to build confidence on known roads
Instructor handling of awkward manoeuvres: provide rationale and break down manoeuvres into smaller steps on request
Jump-start procedure
Tyre checks and changing procedure
Staged progression concept: slow-speed control first then lane discipline (Stage 1 quiet-area drills; Stage 2 residential roads; Stage 3 busy roads).

Consider starting in a quiet space as a preferred initial step
Cost considerations and budgeting for driving lessons
